section.title: Wisdom's Call
section.sectionRationale: This chapter closes out the extended discourses of Proverbs 1 to 9. After repeated warnings against the adulteress and the seductive stranger of chapters 5 to 7, wisdom herself steps into the open, calling publicly at the crossroads and the city gates, and the poem builds to the celebrated claim that wisdom stood beside the LORD before the world was made.
section.thesis: Wisdom is not hidden or scarce; she calls publicly to everyone, offers a treasure greater than any riches, stood at the LORD's side in the making of the world, and delights in the human race, so that to find her is to find life and to reject her is to court death.
section.frameNote: Four movements: wisdom's public call to everyone at the crossroads and gates, verses 1 to 11; the worth of wisdom, her gifts to kings and rulers, riches and righteousness, verses 12 to 21; wisdom present at creation, brought forth before the world and rejoicing in it, verses 22 to 31; and the final appeal, blessed are those who find her, for whoever finds her finds life, verses 32 to 36.

verses.text: Does not {{chokmah|wisdom}} call out, and understanding raise her voice?
On the heights overlooking the road, at the crossroads she takes her stand.
Beside the gates to the city, at the entrances she cries out:
“To you, O men, I call out, and my cry is to the sons of men.
O simple ones, learn to be shrewd; O fools, gain understanding.
Listen, for I speak of noble things, and the opening of my lips will reveal right.
For my mouth will speak the truth, and wickedness is detestable to my lips.
All the words of my mouth are righteous; none are crooked or perverse.
They are all plain to the discerning, and upright to those who find knowledge.
Receive my instruction instead of silver, and knowledge rather than pure gold.
For wisdom is more precious than rubies, and nothing you desire compares with her.

units.label: The worth of wisdom
units.note: better than silver, gold, and rubies

verses.text: I, wisdom, dwell together with prudence, and I find knowledge and discretion.
To {{yirah|fear}} the LORD is to hate evil; I hate arrogant pride, evil conduct, and perverse speech.
Counsel and sound judgment are mine; I have insight and strength.
By me kings reign, and rulers enact just laws;
By me princes rule, and all nobles who govern justly.
I love those who love me, and those who seek me early shall find me.
With me are riches and honor, enduring wealth and righteousness.
My fruit is better than gold, pure gold, and my harvest surpasses choice silver.
I walk in the way of righteousness, along the paths of justice,
bestowing wealth on those who love me and making their treasuries full.
The LORD {{qanah|created}} me as His {{reshit|first course}}, before His works of old.
From everlasting I was established, from the beginning, before the earth began.
When there were no watery depths, I was brought forth, when no springs were overflowing with water.
Before the mountains were settled, before the hills, I was brought forth,
before He made the land or fields, or any of the dust of the earth.
I was there when He established the heavens, when He inscribed a circle on the face of the deep,
when He established the clouds above, when the fountains of the deep gushed forth,
when He set a boundary for the sea, so that the waters would not surpass His command, when He marked out the foundations of the earth.
Then I was a {{amon|skilled craftsman}} at His side, and His delight day by day, rejoicing always in His presence.
I was rejoicing in His whole world, delighting together in the sons of men.
Now therefore, my sons, listen to me, for blessed are those who keep my ways.
Listen to instruction and be wise; do not ignore it.
Blessed is the man who listens to me, watching daily at my doors, waiting at the posts of my doorway.
For whoever finds me finds life and obtains the favor of the LORD.
But he who fails to find me harms himself; all who hate me love death.”

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, the skill of living well before God. In Proverbs 8 wisdom is personified as a woman who calls publicly in the streets, offering herself freely to anyone who will listen, in contrast to the hidden seduction of folly and the adulteress of earlier chapters.

yirah.gloss: The fear of the LORD, reverent submission to God. Verse 13 makes this the moral center of wisdom: to fear the LORD is to hate evil, arrogant pride, and perverse speech, not merely to feel afraid.

qanah.gloss: A verb with a wide range of meaning: to acquire, possess, get, or bring into being. Older English versions render verse 22 'the LORD possessed me'; the BSB and several modern versions render it 'created me'. This range of meaning stands at the center of a famous fourth-century debate over whether the verse teaches that Wisdom, and by extension Christ, was a created being.

reshit.gloss: Beginning, first, or firstfruits. The same word opens the book, 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge', Proverbs 1:7, and here marks wisdom as present at the very start of God's works, before anything else was made.

amon.gloss: A rare and disputed word in verse 30, rendered 'skilled craftsman' or 'master workman' by many translations, though some scholars read it as 'little child', picturing wisdom delighting at the LORD's side like a child at play. Either reading pictures wisdom intimately present with God in creation.

basic.context: Proverbs 8 closes out the extended discourses that open the book, chapters 1 to 9. After warnings against the adulteress and the seductive stranger of chapters 5 to 7, wisdom herself steps into the open: she does not whisper in secret but cries out publicly at the crossroads and the city gates, offering herself freely to anyone who will listen. The chapter builds to a stunning claim: wisdom was present with the LORD before the world was made, and she delights in the human race.
basic.hermeneutics: This is poetry, and wisdom is personified as a woman throughout Proverbs 1 to 9, so the passage should not be read as a literal biography of a separate divine being. At the same time, the poem uses the strongest possible language, wisdom present before creation, brought forth, beside the LORD as He worked, so Christians have long asked how this points forward to Christ, without turning a Hebrew wisdom poem into a proof text for later debates it was not written to settle.
basic.originalLanguages: Wisdom is chokmah, personified as a woman who calls in the street. Verse 13 grounds wisdom in yirah, the fear of the LORD, that hates evil. Verse 22's qanah, created or possessed, is the hinge of a famous ancient debate over Christ's nature. And verse 30's rare amon, skilled craftsman or perhaps little child, pictures wisdom delighting at the LORD's side in creation.
basic.typology: Wisdom brought forth before the world, working at the LORD's side and delighting in humanity, points forward to Christ, through whom all things were made, Colossians 1:15-17, and who is called the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30. John's Gospel makes a similar claim about the Word who was with God in the beginning, John 1:1-3.
basic.christ: Paul calls Christ 'the wisdom of God' and says that in Him are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge, 1 Corinthians 1:24, Colossians 2:3. Whatever this poem meant for its first hearers, the New Testament applies wisdom language to Jesus without hesitation: He is the one through whom and for whom all things were created, and in whom all things hold together, Colossians 1:16-17.
basic.god: God is the master builder who works with wisdom as His skilled companion, delighting in what He makes and especially in the human race, verse 31. He is not a distant architect; creation is joyful, relational work, and He generously offers that same wisdom to anyone who will listen, verses 4 and 17.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 1:24 and 1:30 call Christ the wisdom of God. Colossians 1:15-17 describes Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created. Colossians 2:3 says all the treasures of wisdom are hidden in Him. John 1:1-3 echoes wisdom's presence with God at creation. James 1:5 promises that God gives wisdom generously to those who ask.
basic.oneStory: Wisdom's public call, offering life to whoever will listen, echoes through the whole Bible's invitation to know God, and finds its fullest answer in Christ, the wisdom of God who was with the Father before the world began and who calls all people to Himself, so that whoever finds Him finds life, just as wisdom promises in verse 35.

context.literary: The last and grandest of wisdom's speeches in chapters 1 to 9, structured as a public address: a call to all people, verses 1 to 11; wisdom's own testimony to her worth and public role, verses 12 to 21; a hymn of her presence at creation, verses 22 to 31; and a closing appeal with the stakes of life and death, verses 32 to 36.
context.historical: Set against the backdrop of the ancient Near East, where wisdom was often the guarded possession of scribes, priests, and royal courts, wisdom here calls out in the open, at gates and crossroads, offered freely to ordinary people rather than hoarded by an elite.
context.canonical: This chapter's picture of wisdom present at creation is picked up directly by the New Testament's language for Christ as the agent of creation and the wisdom of God, making it one of the most important Old Testament texts behind later reflection on Christ, while remaining, in its own setting, a poem summoning Israel to choose wisdom over folly.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press home, in the most vivid terms available, that wisdom is not scarce, secret, or reserved for the elite: she calls publicly to everyone, offers more than any riches, and was present with God from before the world began, so that finding her is a matter of life and death.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter's call to seek wisdom and its promise that whoever finds her finds life are direct exhortations. The poetic description of wisdom's presence at creation, verses 22 to 31, is a theological claim about wisdom's origin and worth, not a historical chronicle or a scientific description of creation's order.

debates.question: Who or what is personified Wisdom in Proverbs 8:22-31, and does verse 22 teach that Christ was created?

views.label: A poetic personification of an attribute of God
views.case: Hebrew poetry regularly personifies abstract qualities, folly is also pictured as a woman in chapter 9, so wisdom here is best read as a vivid literary figure for God's own wisdom, not a separate person.

views.label: A hypostasis, a distinct but not fully personal expression of God
views.case: Some interpreters, ancient and modern, read wisdom here as more than a figure of speech, a semi-personal expression of God reaching into creation, anticipating, though not identical with, the New Testament's fuller revelation of the Son.

views.label: Directly the pre-incarnate Christ
views.case: Because the New Testament applies wisdom language to Christ so directly, 1 Corinthians 1:24, 30, Colossians 1:15-17, 2:3, some readers identify the wisdom of Proverbs 8 as the pre-incarnate Son Himself, present and active at creation.

debates.bearing: In the fourth century, Arius appealed to the Septuagint's rendering of verse 22, 'the LORD created me', to argue that the Son was a created being, not eternally divine. Orthodox theologians, including Athanasius, replied that Proverbs 8 is personified Wisdom in a wisdom poem, not a technical statement about the Son's essence, and that Scripture elsewhere teaches the Son is eternally begotten, not made, John 1:1-3, Colossians 1:17. The safest course reads Proverbs 8 as personified wisdom that the New Testament later fills with its fullest meaning in Christ, without treating verse 22 by itself as settling His eternal nature. Handle this text with care and interpretive humility, resisting both a bare literalism that makes Wisdom a created demigod, and an over allegorising that turns an Old Testament wisdom poem into a full treatise on the Trinity.

study.originalLanguages: Wisdom, chokmah, calls publicly in the street, offering herself to anyone who will listen. Verse 13 grounds her in yirah, the fear of the LORD, which hates evil, pride, and perverse speech. Verse 22's qanah, a verb ranging across acquire, possess, and create, is the very word later seized on in the fourth-century Arian controversy. Verse 30's rare amon, skilled craftsman or perhaps little child, pictures wisdom delighting intimately at the LORD's side as He made the world.

typology.type: Wisdom brought forth before creation, Proverbs 8:22-25
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who is before all things, and in whom all things hold together, Colossians 1:17.

typology.type: Wisdom as the LORD's skilled companion in creation, Proverbs 8:30
typology.fulfillment: Christ, through whom all things were made, John 1:3, Colossians 1:16.

typology.type: Wisdom's public call and promise of life, Proverbs 8:1-4, 35
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own public invitation, come to me, and His claim to be the way, the life, and the giver of living water, Matthew 11:28, John 14:6, John 7:37-38.

study.christ: The New Testament takes the language of this chapter and applies it directly to Jesus. Paul calls Christ the wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:24, and says all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge are hidden in Him, Colossians 2:3. Colossians 1:15-17 describes Christ as the one through whom and for whom all things were created and in whom all things hold together, echoing wisdom's role at the LORD's side in Proverbs 8:30. And just as wisdom calls publicly, offering life to whoever will listen, Proverbs 8:1-4, 35, Christ stands and calls, 'Come to me, all who are weary', Matthew 11:28, and 'whoever believes in me will never thirst', John 7:37-38.
study.god: God is the master builder who works with wisdom as His skilled companion, delighting in what He makes and especially in the human race, verse 31. He is not a distant architect; creation is joyful, relational work in this poem, and He generously offers that same wisdom to anyone who will listen, verses 4 and 17, a generosity James later commends to every believer who lacks wisdom, James 1:5.

crossReferences.note: Calls Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.
Christ became for us wisdom from God, and righteousness, sanctification, and redemption.
Christ as the image of the invisible God, the one through whom and for whom all things were created, and in whom all things hold together.
In Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.
The Word was with God in the beginning, and all things were made through Him, echoing wisdom's presence at creation.
God gives wisdom generously to all who ask, without finding fault.

study.oneStory: Wisdom's public call, offering life to whoever will listen and standing at God's side in the making of the world, echoes through the whole Bible's invitation to know God. It finds its fullest answer in Christ, the wisdom of God who was with the Father before the world began and who now calls all people to Himself, so that whoever finds Him finds life, just as wisdom promises in verse 35, and whoever refuses Him harms only himself, as verse 36 warns.

sermon.title: True Wisdom
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through Proverbs 8:10-16 and 22-31, unpacking the importance and definition of wisdom in a pluralistic culture and pointing toward the wisdom the world actually needs, laying groundwork for seeing Christ as the wisdom of God.

quiz.q: Where does wisdom make her public appeal, according to Proverbs 8:1-3?

options.text: At the heights, the crossroads, the city gates, and the entrances, calling out to everyone.
options.note: This matches the chapter's opening scene exactly, wisdom crying out in the most public places available.

options.text: Only privately to kings and rulers inside the palace.
options.note: Wisdom's benefits do extend to kings and rulers, verses 15-16, but her call itself goes out publicly, not privately, first.

options.text: In secret, luring people away the way the adulteress does in earlier chapters.
options.note: This describes the hidden approach of folly and the adulteress in earlier chapters, the very opposite of wisdom's open, public call.

options.text: Nowhere; wisdom stays silent throughout this chapter.
options.note: The opposite is true, wisdom cries out loudly and repeatedly, verses 1 to 4.

quiz.q: According to verses 10 to 11 and 18 to 19, how does wisdom compare to silver, gold, and rubies?

options.text: Wisdom is more precious than any of them; nothing you desire compares with her.
options.note: This is the direct claim of verse 11, repeated in the fruit and harvest imagery of verse 19.

options.text: Wisdom is valuable mainly because the riches it brings matter more than wisdom itself.
options.note: The chapter does promise riches alongside wisdom, verse 18, but it consistently ranks wisdom itself as the greater treasure.

options.text: Wisdom is roughly equal in value to silver and gold.
options.note: The text goes further than equality, calling wisdom more precious than rubies and beyond anything desired.

options.text: Wisdom is worthless compared to material riches.
options.note: This directly contradicts the passage, which exalts wisdom above every treasure named.

quiz.q: What does verse 13 say the fear of the LORD means in practice?

options.text: To hate evil, arrogant pride, evil conduct, and perverse speech.
options.note: This is the exact content of verse 13, defining fear of the LORD by what it hates.

options.text: To avoid making any decision without a priest's approval.
options.note: The verse says nothing about priestly approval; it defines fear of the LORD morally, not procedurally.

options.text: To live in constant terror of God's punishment.
options.note: This misses the verse's own definition, which is about hating evil, not living in dread.

options.text: To focus on outward ritual while ignoring right and wrong.
options.note: This gets the passage backward; verse 13 ties the fear of the LORD directly to moral substance, hating evil, not to ritual observance.

quiz.q: What does Proverbs 8:22-31 claim about wisdom's role at creation, and how does the New Testament develop this?

options.text: Wisdom was present with the LORD before the world began, at His side as He worked and delighting in His creation, a claim the New Testament applies to Christ as the agent of creation, 1 Corinthians 1:24, Colossians 1:16-17.
options.note: This summarizes verses 22-31 and their New Testament development accurately.

options.text: Wisdom was simply one of the created things the LORD made, with no connection drawn anywhere in the New Testament.
options.note: This misses how directly the New Testament connects wisdom's role here to Christ's role in creation.

options.text: Wisdom crushed the head of the serpent as the promised seed of the woman.
options.note: That is the promise of Genesis 3:15, not the subject of Proverbs 8; this passage is about wisdom's presence at creation.

options.text: The passage focuses only on wisdom's usefulness for kings and says nothing about creation.
options.note: Kings are mentioned earlier, verses 15-16, but verses 22-31 specifically describe wisdom's presence at creation, not royal usefulness.

quiz.q: What is the final appeal of the chapter, verses 32 to 36?

options.text: Blessed is the one who listens to wisdom and waits at her doors, for whoever finds her finds life, but whoever fails to find her harms himself.
options.note: This is the exact substance of the closing verses.

options.text: Listening to wisdom is optional and carries no real consequence either way.
options.note: The passage states the opposite, framing the choice in terms of life and death.

options.text: Only those born wise can ever find wisdom; personal effort makes no difference.
options.note: The passage instead urges active listening and daily watching at wisdom's doors, assuming effort matters.

options.text: The chapter ends without hope, since so many fail to find wisdom.
options.note: The ending is genuinely hopeful for those who listen, even though it also warns of the danger in refusing wisdom.

questions: Wisdom cries out publicly, at the crossroads and the gates, not hidden away for a select few. Where in your life have you assumed wisdom, or God Himself, is out of reach, when in truth He is calling to you plainly?

Verse 11 says wisdom is more precious than rubies. If you compared how much time, money, and attention you give to wisdom versus how much you give to comfort or possessions, what would that comparison reveal?
Verse 13 ties the fear of the LORD to hating evil, pride, and perverse speech. Is there a particular sin you have grown comfortable excusing rather than hating?

Proverbs 8:22-31 pictures wisdom delighting in the LORD's presence and in the human race He made. How does it change your view of yourself to know that, in Christ, God delighted in creating and redeeming you?
Wisdom says, those who seek me early shall find me, verse 17. What would it look like to seek God first in your day, rather than after everything else is done?

The chapter ends with a stark choice: find wisdom and find life, or fail to find her and harm yourself. What is one concrete step you can take this week to seek wisdom rather than drift past her call?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew qanah has a wide range, to acquire, possess, get, or bring into being. Older versions such as the KJV render this the LORD possessed me; the BSB and many modern versions render it created me or brought me forth. The Septuagint's created reading became the center of the fourth-century Arian controversy over Christ's nature; orthodox theologians replied that this is personified wisdom in a wisdom poem, not a technical statement about the Son's eternal being.

The BSB's as His first course renders reshit, beginning or first, the same word that opens the book in Proverbs 1:7. Other versions render the phrase in the beginning of his way or the first of his works, all pointing to wisdom's priority before everything else God made.

The rare word amon, rendered skilled craftsman in the BSB, is disputed; some scholars read it as little child, so that wisdom is pictured either as a master workman beside the LORD or as a delighted child at His side. Either reading pictures intimate, joyful nearness to God in the work of creation.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 8:1-36. Three verses in the source, 29, 30, and 34, contained an internal line break for poetic parallelism within a single verse; these were joined with a single space to keep clean single-line verse text, wording otherwise unchanged and complete.
